_**User**_

Rewrite the ## Example section in these note files under /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/

RULES:
- Examples must use ONLY these genres: Sports, Non-technical work environments, Home with Children, Real World environments
- Remove tech-job scenes and aviation; replace PKM-tool scenes with home/commute/sports/non-tech work scenes while keeping the note's claim clear
- Healthcare/school/restaurant/trades = non-technical work (OK)
- Gospel notes: keep faith content but ground scenes in home with children or real world (not tech work)
- Keep JoRap voice: plain words, first person, one concrete scene
- Do NOT change frontmatter, claim paragraph, Note Relationships, or See also

Files needing updates (read each - skip if already fits genres):
analog-capture-tools.md, anti-fragile-systems.md, associative-linking.md, atomic-notes.md, compounding.md, context-aware-capture.md, control-the-rhythm.md, daily-notes.md, deliberate-practice.md, digital-garden.md, digital-serendipity.md, drafting-in-public.md, first-principles-thinking.md, graph-view-analytics.md, gtd-vs-para.md, intellectual-sourcing.md, judge-not.md, let-your-light-shine.md, let-your-yes-be-yes.md, life-before-achievement.md, low-hanging-fruit.md, mental-models.md, metadata-strategy.md, mind-mapping.md, minimum-viable-product.md, note-relationships.md, para-method.md, pareto-principle.md, progressive-summarization.md, reliability.md

mental-models.md currently has a bullet list - rewrite as a concrete scene using one of the four genres.

Return a bullet list of each file changed with the new example text (one line each).

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reading the claim paragraphs in each note file to identify what must be preserved in the rewritten examples.

Evaluating specific scenes in each file. The wiki reference in analog-capture-tools.md needs replacing with a home or commute scene. Checking whether teammate references in reliability.md fit the allowed genres.

Five more files need genre updates: anti-fragile-systems.md, associative-linking.md, atomic-notes.md, compounding.md, and context-aware-capture.md. All contain tech-job or PKM-tool scenes that violate the allowed genres.

Control-the-rhythm.md likely needs a non-tech scene because the client ping resembles office work. Daily-notes.md contains a PKM workflow that must be replaced. Deliberate-practice.md appears acceptable since piano practice and presentations fit the allowed genres.

digital-garden.md, digital-serendipity.md, drafting-in-public.md, first-principles-thinking.md, and graph-view-analytics.md all rely on PKM or tech-job scenes and must be rewritten. The digital-garden example may need a home-journal or community-garden analogy instead of a site-meta reference.

gtd-vs-para.md relies on OmniFocus and PARA folders, so it needs a non-PKM rewrite. intellectual-sourcing.md probably needs a non-tech scene such as homework or recipe research. judge-not.md and let-your-light-shine.md already fit the allowed genres. let-your-yes-be-yes.md uses a "client" reference that likely belongs to tech work and should become a non-tech role like a coach or neighbor.

life-before-achievement.md should swap its generic corporate team-building scene for a school sports example. low-hanging-fruit.md, metadata-strategy.md, and mind-mapping.md all rely on PKM or site-management scenes and need rewriting. mental-models.md currently uses a bullet list and must become a concrete scene.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Reading claim paragraphs for context on the notes that need rewrites.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I have reviewed the requirements. I will now read the remaining claim sections and draft the new examples.

I am reviewing the remaining files, including control-the-rhythm, reliability, and life-before-achievement. For the analog-capture-tools example, I will replace the wiki-based Friday-review scene with a home-based Sunday-night Bible-margin-notes scene to match the allowed genres.

The analog-capture-tools example will describe a phone dying mid-sermon and a pocket notebook capturing Philippians references for Sunday lunch. The anti-fragile-systems example will use a gym training log kept in a notebook when the fitness app crashes.

The analog-capture-tools example now includes a quarterly photocopying step to preserve history across app failures.

For associative-linking, I am weighing two non-technical analogies. One uses a child asking about tablet time and remembering a bedtime link on a fridge chart instead of hunting a folder. The other uses a coach recalling a sprint-versus-marathon note through a contradicts link rather than a folder tree.

The associative-linking example will use a real-world recipe box. A "see also" card on an adobo recipe points to a pressure cooker recipe, avoiding the need to hunt alphabetical folders.

The atomic-notes example will use a carpool scene where a parent leaves cleats by the door. The compounding example will describe a child’s piano practice that seemed pointless for a year.

The compounding example now shows how the child’s piano practice linked in year two, letting her play full songs instead of isolated exercises.

A grocery-list scribble will illustrate context-aware capture: writing “milk — school party Friday” on the receipt back so Saturday’s reminder is not lost.

For control-the-rhythm, the therapist example is being replaced with a coach scene. The coach runs drills and calls water, ending on time, while a parent texts about extra reps before the session even begins.

The control-the-rhythm example now uses a coach who sees Thursday's scrimmage before doubling reps, preventing limp performance.

The daily-notes example uses a pocket slip with fragments that become a family-calendar event if repeated.

The deliberate-practice example will keep the pianist scene.

The digital-garden example will use a community garden where last year's tomato stakes sit beside this year's experiments.

The digital-garden example now uses a community garden that changes its crop with the season, and a little free library with half-finished craft books swapped when a better version appears.

The drafting-in-public example replaces the wiki scene with a rough recipe for arroz caldo posted before the ginger amount was finalized; a comment last month fixed the line that had been dodged.

The first-principles-thinking example now uses a coach scene where a YouTube drill was stripped down to actual passing needs.

The graph-view-analytics example replaces the PKM orphan concept with a kids’ chore chart showing three jobs with zero arrows to anything else.

The gtd-vs-para example contrasts a weekly fridge list with a filing cabinet full of school forms that have no deadlines.

The gtd-vs-para example now adds a household detail: the same household uses two systems, and the author stopped cramming groceries into the permanent folder.

The judge-not and let-your-light-shine examples already fit the allowed genres and will be skipped.

The let-your-yes-be-yes example replaces the client scene with a home non-technical scene. The parent tells a coach he will bring his kid "God willing, absolutely, cross my heart" when a plain yes would have sufficed.

The life-before-achievement example now uses a school cliff-jump field trip instead of a generic corporate scene. The school cancels the cliff-jump when a picnic achieves the same team-building goal without credible risk of injury. Unity does not require a body count.

The low-hanging-fruit example is still being drafted.

The low-hanging-fruit example now uses a spring-cleaning plan that wastes an hour on a small sink of dishes; washing them in ten minutes gives the real win and energy for the garage.

The mental-models example is now a concrete Saturday scene: forty soccer camps overwhelm the parent, but Pareto cuts the choice to three budget camps, low-hanging fruit registers online before slots close, and compounding explains that ten weekly minutes of practice matter more than a flashy jersey.

The metadata-strategy example is still being drafted.

The metadata-strategy example now uses labeled bins for toys, books, and outgrown items, dropping color codes when they become a maintenance burden.

The mind-mapping example uses a messy paper bubble map sketched before a road trip, with each branch becoming its own packing list.

The minimum-viable-product example replaces the tech scene with a birthday party that sat half-planned for three months because the decorations were not yet perfect.

The minimum-viable-product example now uses a birthday party that was finally held with pizza and one banner, giving the parent a real win after months of Pinterest planning.

The note-relationships example uses a family chore chart where "feeds into" and "conflicts with" lines are typed directly onto the chart.

The para-method example uses a school science-fair folder on the counter with a deadline, and immunization records in the filing cabinet with no end date, sorted by ownership and due date rather than alphabet.

The low-hanging-fruit example now uses a kids' activity list trimmed from six to two items so the family stops skipping nights in a row.

The progressive-summarization example uses a PTA newsletter where dates are circled and starred before the sticky note is written to the fridge.

The reliability example uses a friend who shows up at 7:00 instead of saying "around seven," making that friend the go-to contact in a crisis.

The reliability example is now a coach who sends the schedule every Monday and keeps rain practices, not the loudest pep talk.

The deliberate-practice example will keep the pianist scene but add a sports coach briefing line: "A goalkeeper drills the hard save fifty times, not the whole match on autopilot. My kid's recital died in the bridges between songs."

The analog-capture example will drop the wiki reference. The control-the-rhythm example will switch from a client ping to a sports-parent scene. The reliability example will replace the teammate reference with a coach and cut the office-style recap.

The deliberate-practice example will be narrowed to a sports or music scene only, removing any tech references. The anti-fragile example will be rewritten. The compounding example will drop the linked-notes reference because it is PKM. The context-aware example will drop the digital link. The note-relationships example will keep its claim intact while replacing the wikilink table syntax with a concrete scene.

The associative-linking example may keep its wikilink reference, since the note itself is about linking.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Drafting genre-grounded examples and applying the edits.


_**Agent (default)**_

_**Agent (default)**_

**Skipped** (already fit): `judge-not.md`, `let-your-light-shine.md`

**Changed** (28 files):

- **analog-capture-tools.md** — The phone dies mid-sermon and the pocket notebook still works. I scribble Philippians references in the margin and copy them into my study Bible at the kitchen table Sunday night — same captures, no battery required.
- **anti-fragile-systems.md** — When the gym's booking app shut down for good, my paper training log still had every set. I photocopy it once a quarter so I'm not rebuilding history from memory when the next app dies.
- **associative-linking.md** — My kid asks why we can't sprint through bedtime and I don't remember which card says "meltdown." The arrow from "rush" to "slow the moment" on the fridge chart gets me there — not the folder tab labeled "evening."
- **atomic-notes.md** — "Why cleats live by the door" is one claim — I'd say it alone at carpool. "Soccer gear and lunch-packing tips" is two — split them and link the ideas.
- **compounding.md** — My kid's piano practice looked pointless for a year — three songs a week, same scales. Year two the pieces started linking and suddenly she was playing music, not exercises. The first months looked like nothing; boring Tuesdays bent the curve.
- **context-aware-capture.md** — At the grocery I scribble "milk — school party Friday" on the list back. Saturday that line is the only reason I remember it wasn't just breakfast milk.
- **control-the-rhythm.md** — Good coaches don't let the loudest parent set practice length — they run drills, call water, end on time. A dad texted "my kid needs more reps today" before I'd watched him play. Control the rhythm was "let's see Thursday's scrimmage first" instead of doubling reps that bred a limp.
- **daily-notes.md** — Today's pocket slip is just fragments: pick up cleats, dentist Tuesday, call Mom back. I promote one line to the family calendar Sunday if it keeps showing up; the rest can fade.
- **deliberate-practice.md** — A goalkeeper drills the hard save fifty times, not the whole match on autopilot. My kid's recital died in the bridges between songs. Deliberate practice was running only those transitions ten times — the rest she already knew.
- **digital-garden.md** — Our block's little free library still has last year's craft books beside this year's swaps. I replace a title when I learn better — not when the first donation was supposed to be final.
- **digital-serendipity.md** — I'm flipping through old recipe cards while packing lunch and a card on quick weeknight meals bumps into one I wrote last month on batch cooking. I didn't plan the meeting — the "see also" arrow on the adobo card did.
- **drafting-in-public.md** — I posted a rough arroz caldo recipe before I'd nailed the ginger amount because silence let me pretend I understood it. A neighbor's comment last month fixed the line I'd been dodging.
- **first-principles-thinking.md** — My kid's coach kept quoting a YouTube drill until I asked what problem we're actually solving — turns out we needed passing, not fancy footwork. That rewrite was stripping borrowed moves down to what our team lacks.
- **graph-view-analytics.md** — I laid out the kids' chore chart and three jobs had zero arrows to anything else — nobody knew who checked them. That gap list was my fix-this-week, not decoration.
- **gtd-vs-para.md** — The fridge list holds what we buy this week; the filing cabinet holds school forms with no deadline. Same household, two systems — I stopped cramming groceries into the permanent folder.
- **intellectual-sourcing.md** — I tape the newspaper name under the quote on my kid's history project so six months later we know if the source still says what we thought. Without that line our own summary starts to feel like gospel.
- **let-your-yes-be-yes.md** — I told the coach I'd have my kid there "God willing, absolutely, cross my heart" when a plain yes would have been enough. If my word needs extra padding to sound believable, the problem isn't the schedule — it's me.
- **life-before-achievement.md** — The school canceled the cliff-jump field trip when the park picnic hit the same bonding goal without a credible risk of a broken leg. Unity doesn't require a stunt.
- **low-hanging-fruit.md** — I spent an hour sketching a whole spring-cleaning plan when the actual problem was three dishes in the sink. I washed them in ten minutes, got the win, and had energy for the garage after lunch.
- **mental-models.md** — Saturday soccer signup had forty camps listed and I froze. Pareto got me to three that fit our budget; low-hanging fruit was registering online before slots closed; compounding was the ten minutes a week of practice that'll matter more than the flashiest jersey.
- **metadata-strategy.md** — I label the kids' bins "toys," "books," "outgrown" — not a color code I'd have to maintain like a second job. If sorting takes longer than putting stuff away, I cut a category.
- **mind-mapping.md** — I sketch a messy bubble map on paper before our road trip, then each branch becomes its own packing list. The map is the brainstorm; the checklist on the fridge is the storage.
- **minimum-viable-product.md** — I sat on a half-planned birthday party for three months because the decorations weren't perfect. We ordered pizza, put out one banner, and the kids had a blast — I learned more than another month of Pinterest would've.
- **note-relationships.md** — I'm updating the family chore chart and add "feeds into: Saturday yard work" next to "take out trash" plus "conflicts with: bedtime when done late" before I tape it to the fridge. Two rows, named tension, everyone can read it.
- **para-method.md** — The science fair folder lives on the counter with a deadline; the immunization records live in the filing cabinet with no end date. I sort by what owns it and when it's due, not alphabet soup.
- **pareto-principle.md** — Last month the kids' activity list had six things and we skipped three nights in a row. I trimmed to two — soccer and piano — and suddenly we were showing up again instead of guilt-driving past canceled plans.
- **progressive-summarization.md** — I open the PTA newsletter for the second time and circle three dates, then star one. The sticky on the fridge only gets written when I'm actually blocking the calendar.
- **reliability.md** — The friend who shows up at 7:00, not "around seven," is who you call in a crisis. My kid's coach sends the schedule every Monday and doesn't ghost when it's rain practice — not the loudest pep talk, the one whose yes means yes.
